---
title: "Crime Trend Analysis and Forecasting in Pakistan"
subtitle: "A Statistical and Machine Learning Approach Using R"
author: "Manzoor Ali"
date: "`r format(Sys.Date(), '%B %Y')`"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    theme: flatly
  word_document:
    toc: true
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE, fig.align = "center")
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(forecast)
```

# 1. Introduction

Rising and shifting crime patterns present a major challenge for policymakers
and law-enforcement resource planning in Pakistan. This project analyzes
month-wise national crime data (2022-2024, sourced from the Ministry of
Interior's National Police Bureau) across 11 crime categories, applying a
full statistical and machine learning pipeline in R: data cleaning,
exploratory analysis, statistical testing, regression, clustering, time
series decomposition, and ARIMA/ETS forecasting.

# 2. Data

```{r load-data}
crime      <- read.csv("data/crime_transformed.csv", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
monthly    <- read.csv("data/monthly_total.csv", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
desc_stats <- read.csv("data/descriptive_stats.csv", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
```

The cleaned dataset covers `r length(unique(crime$Category))` crime
categories over `r nrow(monthly)` months (January 2022 - December 2024).
Note: "Rape" and "Gang Rape" appear as distinct police-reported categories
only from 2024 onward, reflecting a change in reporting scope rather than
missing data in prior years.

# 3. Exploratory Data Analysis

Total reported crime rose from **1,119,817** cases in 2022 to **1,467,205**
in 2023 and **1,511,238** in 2024 - a **~35% increase** over the two years
following 2022. Seasonal peaks recur around **March** and **August-September**
each year, while **January/February** tend to show relative troughs.

![Total Monthly Crime Trend](figures/01_total_trend.png)

![Seasonality by Calendar Month](figures/05_month_boxplot.png)

"Miscellaneous" and "Other Theft" dominate total case volume, while violent
crimes (Murder, Kidnapping/Abduction) show smaller but steadily rising counts.

![Category Totals](figures/03_category_totals.png)

# 4. Statistical Analysis

```{r desc-stats}
knitr::kable(desc_stats, digits = 1)
```

A one-way ANOVA of monthly case counts against **Year** and against
**calendar Month** was used to test whether year-level and seasonal effects
are statistically significant; correlation analysis shows property crimes
(Robbery, Burglary, Other Theft) move closely together (r > 0.8), while
violent crimes form a separate correlated cluster.

![Correlation Heatmap](figures/09_correlation_heatmap.png)

# 5. Regression Analysis

A linear trend model (`Cases ~ TimeIndex`) estimates the average month-on-month
increase in total crime, and an extended model adds calendar-month dummies to
capture seasonality. See `scripts/06_regression_analysis.R` and
`data/regression_summary.txt` for full model output and coefficients.

![Regression Fit](figures/30_regression_fit.png)

# 6. Clustering

K-means clustering (k = 3, selected via the elbow method) was applied to the
standardized monthly profiles of each crime category, grouping categories by
similarity in scale and seasonal behavior rather than raw volume.

![Cluster Visualization](figures/18_cluster_pca.png)

Three clusters emerge: (1) low-to-moderate volume violent/property crimes
that move together, (2) "Other Theft" as a high-volume outlier, and
(3) "Miscellaneous" as the largest, most distinct category.

# 7. Time Series Analysis

Seasonal decomposition separates the series into trend, seasonal, and
residual components. An Augmented Dickey-Fuller test on the raw series
was not significant at the 5% level, but the **first-differenced** series
is stationary (p < 0.001), justifying an ARIMA(p,1,q) specification.

![Seasonal Decomposition](figures/10_decomposition.png)

# 8. Forecasting (ARIMA & ETS)

Both models were fit on data through June 2024 and evaluated on the
remaining 6 held-out months.

```{r forecast-accuracy}
comparison <- read.csv("data/forecast_accuracy_comparison.csv")
knitr::kable(comparison, digits = 2)
```

**ARIMA outperformed ETS** on the holdout set (lower RMSE and MAPE),
so the final 6-month-ahead forecast uses the ARIMA specification refit
on the full 2022-2024 series.

![ARIMA Forecast](figures/21_arima_forecast.png)
![ETS Forecast](figures/22_ets_forecast.png)

```{r future-forecast}
future <- read.csv("data/future_forecast_6months.csv")
knitr::kable(future, digits = 0)
```

# 9. Conclusion

Total reported crime in Pakistan trended upward over 2022-2024 with
consistent seasonal peaks in March and August-September. Property crimes
(Robbery, Burglary, Other Theft) are highly correlated and cluster together,
suggesting shared underlying drivers, while violent crime categories show
steadier, more predictable patterns. ARIMA(0,1,1)-family models provide the
most accurate short-term forecasts, projecting continued elevated crime
levels over the next 6 months, useful for law-enforcement resource planning
and seasonal deployment decisions.

# 10. Limitations

- Only 36 monthly observations limits the precision of seasonal ARIMA/ETS
  estimation; longer historical data would improve forecast reliability.
- "Rape" and "Gang Rape" are only available from 2024, preventing full
  3-year trend analysis for those categories.
- Data reflects **reported** cases only, not actual crime incidence.
